title: Lemon curd pavlova
subtitle: Large meringue topped with whipped cream and sharp lemon curd.
excerpt: Lemon curd pavlova replaces the usual berry topping with spoonfuls of tart lemon curd. The sharp, egg-thickened curd cuts through the sweet meringue in a way fresh fruit can't quite match. The cream and curd can be layered or swirled together. The meringue itself is the same soft, marshmallow-centred base as any pavlova.
sourceNotes: Pavlova as a form is well documented in Australian and New Zealand baking from the 1930s onwards. The lemon curd topping is a variant that became popular in British food media in the 1990s and 2000s, combining the pavlova's Australian origin with the British tradition of lemon curd as a preserve. Lemon curd itself appears in British cookbooks from at least the early nineteenth century. This recipe uses bought lemon curd for simplicity; the meringue method follows the New Zealand tradition with cornflour and vinegar for a marshmallow centre.

recipe.freezeNotes: Meringue does not freeze. Store the unfilled meringue shell in a dry place at room temperature for up to 24 hours.
recipe.makeAheadNotes: The meringue shell keeps well overnight in a dry spot. Assemble no more than 30 minutes before serving as lemon curd is wetter than fresh fruit.

recipeTools.notes: Or a hand mixer. Bowl must be grease-free.

content.text: Lemon curd and meringue are a natural pairing: the acidity of the curd slices through the meringue's sweetness in a way that softer fruit cannot. This version uses bought lemon curd, which is consistent and sharp. If you make your own, let it cool completely before spreading it on the cream, or it will melt the topping.
Assemble close to serving time. Lemon curd is more liquid than berries and will start to bleed through the cream after about 30 minutes.

items.prepNote: from large eggs, at room temperature

content.text: Heat the oven to 130°C fan (150°C conventional). Line a baking tray with baking paper and mark a 22 cm circle. Flip the paper.
Whisk {{egg-whites}} in a clean, dry bowl on medium-high speed to stiff peaks.

Add {{caster-sugar}} a tablespoon at a time with the mixer running, waiting 10 seconds between each addition. Beat until the meringue is thick, glossy, and smooth between your fingers (about 10 minutes total).
Fold in {{cornflour}} and {{white-wine-vinegar}} with a spatula.

Spoon onto the lined tray within the circle, building the edges slightly higher than the centre. Bake for 70 minutes, then turn off the oven and leave the pavlova with the door ajar for at least 2 hours.

To assemble: whip {{double-cream}} to soft peaks. Spread over the pavlova. Spoon {{lemon-curd}} in generous dollops over the cream; swirl lightly with a spoon. Scatter lemon zest over the top if using. Serve within 30 minutes.

attrs.heading: Troubleshooting
attrs.intro: Same meringue rules as any pavlova.

items.symptom: Lemon curd sinking through the cream into the meringue.
items.cause: Curd warm, or cream not firm enough.
items.fix: Use lemon curd cold from the fridge. Whip the cream to soft peaks; if it is barely whipped, the curd slides straight through.

items.cause: Humid conditions, or meringue not cooled completely before removing.
items.fix: In humid weather, keep the meringue in the switched-off oven until just before assembly. Humidity causes meringue to weep.
